title: Quiz 1
text:
Quiz 1
This quiz will be held in the first 10 minutes of lab on Thursday. It will contain two questions:

What is the definition of Statistics?
What are the five steps in the Statistical Process?

Normally I’ll provide both practice problems and their answers to help prepare for our weekly quizzes. But because this quiz is our first one and you won’t have had enough time to properly prepare, it will be graded based on participation, not correctness.

title: Quiz 10
text:
These practice problems cover Lecture 25 (one numerical variable across groups: spread, boxplots, group summaries; mean vs median as summaries of center), Lecture 26 (two numerical variables: scatterplots, form / direction / strength), Lecture 27 (two categorical variables: two-way tables, joint / row / column proportions, stacked bar charts), and Lab 10 (missing data, read_csv, bivariate plots in R).





Work without notes first, then check your work.
For multiple-choice items, choose the best single answer.
You may use a scientific calculator on this quiz and your hand draw “methods” table (aka our statistical “toolbox” organized by purpose and type of data).





The figures below are for practice only (made-up teaching data). In each part, answer the multiple-choice questions.







Practice scatterplot for Quiz 10.




(a) Which phrase best describes the direction of the association?
a) Positive (as X increases, Y tends to increase).
b) Negative (as X increases, Y tends to decrease).
c) No clear direction.
(b) Which phrase best describes strength?
a) Strong (points hug a clear trend).
b) Weak (almost no pattern).
c) Moderate (a visible trend with noticeable scatter).
(c) Which phrase best describes form?
a) Roughly linear.
b) Clearly curved (nonlinear).
c) No simple form.









Side-by-side boxplots for two groups.




(a) Which group has the larger median?
a) Control
b) Treatment
c) They are essentially the same.
(b) Which group’s IQR appears to be larger?
a) Control
b) Treatment
c) Hard to tell from the plot.









Stacked counts: habitat by species.




Each bar is a species; segments are Urban vs Rural; height of a segment is the count in that cell.
(a) The total height of the Robin bar equals which quantity?
a) The number of Robins (Urban + Rural).
b) The proportion of all birds that are Robins.
c) The number of Urban birds of all species.
(b) The height of the Urban segment in the Robin bar divided by the full height of the Robin bar equals which quantity?
a) A joint proportion (that cell count ÷ total birds of all species).
b) A row proportion (Urban among Robins).
c) A column proportion (Robins among Urban birds).




(a) Suppose you are writing a report on that uses data on crop weights harvested from many small plots. Rather than report each individual weight, you want to report one summary number. You think that readers might want to estimate the overall tonnage from a set of plots. Which summary is more appropriate to report?

a) Median weight of crops from your plots
b) Mean weight of crops from your plots
c) The range of weights of crops from your plots
(b) Suppose you are summarizing home sale prices in a city where most houses sell for similar amounts but a few are very expensive. You want a single number that reflects what feels like a typical home price for a buyer, not a number dominated by those few extremes. Which is usually more appropriate to report?

a) Mean price.
b) Median price.
c) The minimum price only.





For each pair of variables, pick the best primary plot from the list (assume you want one clear graphic for a report):
Choices for plotting two variables: (same for each question):
a) Scatterplot (geom_point)
b) Boxplot per category (geom_boxplot with fill or group)
c) Histogram with groups overlaid or faceted (geom_histogram with fill or group)
d) Stacked bar chart (geom_col with fill or group)


Variable 1: stem diameter (cm)
Variable 2: forest type (pine, oak, mixed)




Variable 1: soil nitrate (mg/kg)
Variable 2: leaf nitrogen (%)




Variable 1: island (Newberg, Erie, Coney).
Variable 2: species (blackbeak, redbeak, bluebeak)




Variable 1: body mass (g)
Variable 2: coast (west, east) and You want to compare the shape of the mass distribution between the two coasts.







What does read_csv("https://example.com/data.csv") (with a valid URL) do in R?
a) It reads a “colon separated value” file and replaces all the colons with dashes.
b) It reads a CSV file from the web into R as a data frame–like object (typically a tibble).
c) It deletes all missing values in the dataset and then loads the result as a macaroon.
d) It only works on files you already uploaded to Google Drive.




Suppose we write penguins |> drop_na(species, bill_length_mm). What is the main effect?
a) It drops the columns species and bill_length_mm from the penguins dataset.
b) It removes every row that has an NA in either species or bill_length_mm (or both).
c) It replaces any NA in either species or bill_length_mm with zero.
d) It keeps only rows in penguins where both variables species and bill_length_mmare not NA.







All plants in a small plot are classified by shade (Sun, Shade) and height class (Short, Tall). The counts are:




Short
Tall
Row total

Shade

40 (grand total \(N\))



Compute the four joint proportions. Check that they sum to \(1\).




Use the same table as in D1 and compute
(a) Row proportions: what fraction of Sun plants are Short, and what fraction are Tall?
(b) Column proportions: what fraction of Short plants are in Sun, and what fraction are in Shade?







The sorted leaf length values (cm) for \(n = 9\) plants are:
\[2,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6,\ 7,\ 8,\ 9,\ 11,\ 15\]
Use the same splitting rule as in Lecture 25 for odd \(n\): the median is the middle value; the lower half and upper half for quartiles exclude the median (four values in each half); for four numbers, \(Q_1\) and \(Q_3\) are the averages of the two middle values in each half.
(a) Report the five-number summary: min, \(Q_1\), median, \(Q_3\), max.

title: Quiz 11
text:
These practice problems cover Lecture 28 and Lecture 29.





Work without the solutions first, then check your work in Canvas.







Researchers randomly assign volunteers with mild seasonal allergies to use either a new nasal spray (Spray) or an identical-looking placebo (Control) for four weeks. They record whether each person reports “clear improvement” (yes/no). Which description best fits this study?

Randomized controlled experiment (RCT)

Observational study

Simple Random Sampling

Parameter





Suppose the spray group has a higher rate of “clear improvement” than the control group, and a statistical test yields a small p-value. In one sentence, what explanation does the p-value address? (Choose the best option.)

The probability that the spray causes improvement in any one person.

How compatible the data are with a “by chance alone” explanation for the observed difference between groups, under the test’s assumptions and null hypothesis.

Proof that no confounding occurred.

The probability that the null hypothesis is true.





A newspaper headline reads: “People who drink more green tea live longer, study finds.” The underlying study measured tea intake and survival in a large observational study (no random assignment of tea drinking). Which conclusion is most appropriate for the researchers to emphasize in the discussion?

Green tea caused longer life in the study population.

Higher tea intake is linked with longer survival; a causal interpretation requires ruling out confounding and other biases, which this design does not do by itself.

Because the sample was large, causation is established.
It’s not ethical to force people to drink green tea so this study provides high quality evidence by allowing people to chose to drink green tea or not.






Which design most directly supports causal language about the effect of a treatment on an outcome (for people like those enrolled)?

Volunteers choose treatment A vs B; outcomes are compared.

A randomized controlled trial.

A survey asking people whether they used treatment A and whether they feel better.

A chart review comparing outcomes at two hospitals that use different protocols, with no randomization.





Describe the main difference between an observational study and a randomized controlled trial (RCT) when comparing an explanatory variable (treatment or exposure) to a response variable, focusing on how participants end up in the comparison groups.




A statistical analysis finds an association between an explanatory variable \(X\) and a response \(Y\).
(a) If the study is observational, list at least three kinds of explanations (use short labels or phrases) for why \(X\) and \(Y\) might appear to be linked
(b) If the study is a well-conducted RCT comparing assigned treatment to control and a difference in \(Y\) is observed, what two broad explanations are possible?







A confounder is best described as a variable that….




Researchers compare mean blood pressure between two clinics. Clinic A has lower mean blood pressure than Clinic B. They ignore age: Clinic A serves many young adults; Clinic B serves many older adults, and age is strongly related to blood pressure. Age here acts as:

The primary outcome of interest.

A confounder of the clinic–blood pressure association.

A mediator caused by clinic that explains blood pressure.

Random chance only.





Why does random assignment of treatment in an RCT reduce the risk of confounding for the treatment–outcome comparison (as taught in Lecture 28), compared with letting people choose their treatment?

Randomization guarantees there are zero confounders.

Randomization tends to balance both measured and unmeasured baseline factors across arms on average, so treatment groups are more comparable at the start.

Randomization removes the need to record the outcome.

Randomization replaces the need for any statistical test.





Two variables are associated in the data. You are told the explanatory variable causes a third variable, and that this third variable causes the response variable, and the explanatory variable does not directly cause the response except through that third variable. That third variable is:

A confounder.

Not a confounder.





Observational study. Researchers record whether adults choose to take a daily vitamin D supplement (\(X\)) and measure femoral neck bone density as a numerical response \(Y\) (same clinic visit as supplement status).
Which of the following variables could be a confounder of the association between \(X\) and \(Y\)? Select all that apply.

Age (related to both supplement habits and bone density)

Femoral neck bone density \(Y\) itself

Overall diet quality

Sex assigned at birth

Parathyroid hormone (PTH) measured at the same visit, on the biological pathway by which vitamin D status influences bone turnover





Observational study. \(X\) = whether a child’s home is within 500 m of a busy road; \(Y\) = whether the child has ever been given a diagnosis of asthma by a clinician.
Which of the following could be a confounder of an observed link between \(X\) and \(Y\)? Select all that apply.

Household income (related to which neighborhoods families can afford and to healthcare access)

The child’s asthma diagnosis \(Y\)

Parental cigarette smoking in the home

Traffic-related air pollution concentration estimated at the home (often “near road → pollution → asthma”)

Child’s age in years





You are analyzing an observational study with an explanatory variable \(X\) and a response \(Y\). You identify a variable \(C\) that could be a confounder, and you can measure \(C\) for everyone in the dataset. Which option best describes a central idea of subclassification?

Drop all rows with \(C\) missing and rerun only a p-value on the full sample.

Split the data by the categories of \(C\) and compare the \(X\)–\(Y\) relationship within each level of \(C\) (same value of the confounder), e.g. tables or plots or models separately in each category of \(C\).

Replace \(X\) with \(C\) so the analysis no longer mentions the explanatory variable.

Collapse \(Y\) and \(C\) into a single composite score before making any plots or computing a p-value.








A campus survey finds that students who report more hours of sleep also report higher GPAs. A mattress company blog claims: “Sleep more and your grades will improve.” Write two or three sentences explaining why this claim may be too strong, naming at least one alternative explanation besides “sleep causes GPA”.




An observational study finds that people who enroll in a free fitness program have better cardiovascular health one year later than people who do not enroll. The program is not randomized. Give one reason why the conclusion “the program caused better health” might be unwarranted without further evidence.

title: Quiz 12
text:
These practice problems cover Lecture 31 (study design and inference language: random sample vs convenience sample; randomized experiment vs observational study; causation, confounding, and chance), Lecture 32 (one-way ANOVA concepts and interpreting overall F-test output), and Lecture 33 (chi-square test of independence concepts and interpreting overall test output).
This quiz is concepts only: no hand-calculation is required.





Work without notes first, then check your work.
For multiple-choice items, choose the best single answer.
Unless a question says otherwise, interpret significance at alpha = 0.05.





For each scenario, classify whether observations are paired/dependent or independent/not paired.


Thirty students have their resting heart rate measured before and after a 6-week exercise program. Their measurements before and after are:

Paired/dependent by student

Independent/not paired within students





Two separate sections of STAT 109 take different versions of a quiz. Each student appears in only one section. The quiz scores are:

Paired/dependent between the sections

Independent/not paired between the sections





Researchers match each smoker with one nonsmoker of similar age and sex, then compare lung function between matched pairs. The measurements of lung function are:

Paired/dependent between the groups of smokers and non-smokers

Independent/not paired between the groups of smokers and non-smokers





Plant height is measured for seedlings randomly assigned to fertilizer A, B, or C, with each seedling receiving only one fertilizer. The plant height data is:

Paired/dependent between the fertilizer types

Independent/not paired between the fertilizer types








Suppose boxplots are used to compare blood-pressure reduction across three treatments. The medians are visibly different, and one group is shifted noticeably higher than the other two.
Which statement is best?

The boxplot is descriptive evidence of at least one group mean difference.

The boxplot proves all population means are different.

The boxplot shows no evidence of any differences.





You fit fit <- lm(y ~ group, data = dat) and run anova(fit). The output shows a p-value = 0.012.
At alpha = 0.05, which interpretation is best?

Reject H0; the data provide evidence that at least one population mean differs beyond chance variation under the model assumptions.

Fail to reject H0; all means are exactly equal.

Reject H0; therefore every pair of means is significantly different.





A 100% stacked bar chart shows disease status (yes/no) by exposure group (low/medium/high). The segment proportions differ clearly across exposure groups.
Which statement is best?

This is descriptive evidence variables may not be independent.

This proves causation by exposure.

This shows the two variables must be independent.





You run chisq.test(tab) and see p-value = 0.18.
At alpha = 0.05, which conclusion is best?

Reject H0; there is strong evidence of association.

Fail to reject H0; sample data are compatible with independence (not enough evidence of association).

Accept H0 as true with certainty.








For a one-way ANOVA with 4 groups, which is the correct null?

H0: all four population means are equal.

H0: all four sample means are equal.

H0: at least one population mean differs.





For a chi-square test of independence between treatment arm and side-effect category:

H0: treatment arm and side-effect category are independent in the population.

H0: treatment arm causes side effects.

H0: every cell count in the sample table is equal.





Complete the statement:
“A small p-value means that, if the null were true, data this extreme would be _______.”

likely

uncommon (unlikely)

impossible





Which sentence is best statistical wording when p = 0.003 for an ANOVA overall test?

“There is strong evidence against equal means; such differences would be unlikely by chance alone if all means were equal.”

“The null is impossible and chance never plays a role.”

“This proves treatment mechanism with no assumptions.”








A researcher recruits volunteers from people walking into one gym between 5-6 pm and surveys protein intake.
Can the results be safely generalized to all adults in the city?

Yes, because sample size is all that matters.

Not reliably; this is a convenience sample and may not represent the city population.

Yes, because any sample supports broad generalization.





A simple random sample (SRS) is drawn from a complete city resident list.
Which statement is best?

Generalization to that city population is as safe as we can manage from limited information

No generalization is ever possible, even from SRS.

SRS automatically proves causation.








In an RCT, participants are randomly assigned to drug vs placebo. The drug group has a lower mean symptom score and a hypothesis test comparing the population means has a very small p-value.
Which interpretation is best?

Random assignment helps support a causal interpretation

Because chance exists, random assignment gives no advantage.

RCTs cannot address causation.





In an observational study, soda intake is positively associated with BMI.
Which interpretation is best?

title: Quiz 12
text:

Which statement is best?

The boxplot is descriptive evidence of at least one group mean difference.

The boxplot proves all population means are different.

The boxplot shows no evidence of any differences.





You fit fit <- lm(y ~ group, data = dat) and run anova(fit). The output shows a p-value = 0.012.
At alpha = 0.05, which interpretation is best?

Reject H0; the data provide evidence that at least one population mean differs beyond chance variation under the model assumptions.

Fail to reject H0; all means are exactly equal.

Reject H0; therefore every pair of means is significantly different.





A 100% stacked bar chart shows disease status (yes/no) by exposure group (low/medium/high). The segment proportions differ clearly across exposure groups.
Which statement is best?

This is descriptive evidence variables may not be independent.

This proves causation by exposure.

This shows the two variables must be independent.





You run chisq.test(tab) and see p-value = 0.18.
At alpha = 0.05, which conclusion is best?

Reject H0; there is strong evidence of association.

Fail to reject H0; sample data are compatible with independence (not enough evidence of association).

Accept H0 as true with certainty.

title: Quiz 12
text:

likely

uncommon (unlikely)

impossible

“The null is impossible and chance never plays a role.”

“This proves treatment mechanism with no assumptions.”

SRS automatically proves causation.

title: Quiz 13
text:
These practice problems cover Lecture 35 and Lecture 36:

reading scatterplots for form, direction, and strength,
Pearson correlation and simple linear regression,
interpreting slope/intercept and model predictions,
residuals, training vs test MSE and R^2,
interpreting confidence intervals and prediction intervals from R output.

This quiz is primarily conceptual and interpretation-focused (no long hand calculations).





Work without notes first, then check your work.
For multiple-choice questions, choose the best single answer.
Unless stated otherwise, interpret intervals at 95% confidence.







A scatterplot of y vs x shows points roughly around an upward straight-line trend with moderate scatter.
Best description:

Linear, negative, weak

Linear, positive, moderate

Curved, positive, strong





Which value of Pearson correlation r best matches a strong negative linear association?

r = -0.86

r = 0.08

r = 0.74





Which statement about correlation is correct?

Correlation implies causation.

Correlation only captures linear association and is unitless.

Correlation is measured in units of y.






Suppose the fitted line is:
\[\hat{y} = 42.5 + 3.1x\]


Interpret the slope:

For each 1-unit increase in x, predicted mean y increases by about 3.1 units.

When x = 0, predicted y is 3.1.

The model explains 3.1% of variation in y.





Interpret the intercept:

For each 1-unit increase in x, predicted mean y changes by 42.5.

Predicted mean y when x = 0 is 42.5 (if meaningful in context).

The minimum possible value of y is 42.5.





Use the model to predict y when x = 10.

42.5 + 3.1(10) = 73.5

42.5 + 10 = 52.5

3.1 + 10 = 13.1








A residual is:

predicted - observed

observed - predicted

observed / predicted





Why is training MSE often optimistically biased?

It is measured on the same data used to fit the model.

Test sets always have data-entry errors.

MSE cannot be computed on training data.





Which metric is usually better for expected performance on new data?

Training MSE

Test MSE

Training residual mean





A model has test R^2 = 0.64. Best interpretation:

64% of variability in x is explained by y.

About 64% of variability in test-set y is explained by linear prediction from x.

64% of individual predictions are exact.






Suppose R output includes:

Coefficients:
(Intercept) Estimate = 50.2
hours Estimate = 2.8
Pr(>|t|) for hours = 0.004
Multiple R-squared: 0.58



What does Estimate = 2.8 for hours mean?

Predicted mean outcome increases by about 2.8 units per +1 hour in x.

Outcome is always exactly 2.8.

Correlation equals 2.8.





How should you interpret Pr(>|t|) = 0.004 for hours?

Strong evidence slope differs from 0 (under model assumptions).

Exactly 0.4% of observations are errors.

Probability the null is true is 0.004.





How should you interpret Multiple R-squared: 0.58?

58% of x is explained by y.

About 58% of variation in y in the fitted sample is explained by the linear model.

58% chance the model is correct.








Suppose confint(fit, "hours") returns (1.2, 4.3).
Best interpretation:

We are 95% confident the population slope is between 1.2 and 4.3.

95% of points lie between 1.2 and 4.3.

The slope equals exactly 2.75 with certainty.





predict(fit, newdata = data.frame(hours = 8), interval = "confidence") gives:

fit = 72.1, lwr = 69.8, upr = 74.4

Interpretation:

95% CI for mean outcome among all units with hours = 8 is 69.8 to 74.4.

95% of individual outcomes at hours = 8 must be in 69.8 to 74.4.

72.1 is the slope.





predict(fit, newdata = data.frame(hours = 8), interval = "prediction") gives:

fit = 72.1, lwr = 60.4, upr = 83.8

Best interpretation:

95% interval for one future individual outcome at hours = 8 is 60.4 to 83.8.

95% CI for the mean at hours = 8 is 60.4 to 83.8.

The model is poor because the interval is wider than a mean-response CI.





Why is a prediction interval usually wider than a confidence interval for the mean at the same x0?

It includes both uncertainty in the mean and individual-level variability.

It uses a different unit system.

It always uses a larger sample size.








Which condition is important for using usual t-based confidence intervals for slope in simple linear regression?

Residuals are roughly normal (especially for small/moderate sample sizes).

Explanatory variable is categorical.

Response must be binary.





A residual histogram is strongly skewed with extreme outliers in a small sample. Best response:

Interpret CIs/p-values cautiously; model assumptions may be questionable.

Ignore diagnostics if R^2 is high.

Conclude slope is definitely zero.








Explain the difference between what test MSE and test R^2 each tell you.




In plain language, explain what it means when the 95% CI for slope excludes 0.




A student says: “Our training R^2 is high, so we are guaranteed to predict new data well.” Briefly respond.




You are given both a confidence interval for the mean at x0 and a prediction interval at the same x0. Which should you report for:

a population-average statement, and
an individual future-case statement?

text:
These practice problems cover:

Lecture 13: ESP worksheet — stating hypotheses, verifying BRP conditions, identifying \(n\) and \(p\), and finding the p-value.

Work without notes first; then check your answers against the solutions in Canvas.
For graph questions: The figures show the null distribution (bar plot of \(x\) vs \(P(X=x)\)) with no shading. On each figure, by hand: (1) draw a vertical line at the observed value of \(x\) given in the problem, and (2) shade the bar(s) that correspond to the p-value (in the direction of \(H_a\)).
The in-person quiz in lab will be created from these practice problems.







Research question (in words): We want to test whether a person has Extra Sensory Perception (ESP) — the ability to predict outcomes better than chance. To do this, we use an online test: on each of 25 trials, the computer randomly shows one of 5 possible symbols (e.g. circle, cross, star), and the person guesses which symbol it is. If they do not have ESP, they are just guessing among 5 options on each trial. If they do have ESP, we expect them to get more than chance correct.
Let \(p\) = probability of a correct prediction on a single trial (when the person makes one guess).

Write the null hypothesis \(H_0\) and alternative hypothesis \(H_a\) in symbols.
In words, what does it mean if \(H_0\) is true? What are we claiming if we believe \(H_a\)?

In context, what would the Type I error be?

text:

confidence intervals for one mean and one proportion,

NoteQuick reference for Quiz 8





\[
\bar{x} \pm t^* \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}
\]

Statistic: \(\bar{x}\)

SE: \(s/\sqrt{n}\)

Conditions: random sample (or SRS), and population roughly normal or \(n\) moderately large




\[
\bar{x} \pm t^* \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}
\]

Use when population is finite and \(n/N\) is not small.




\[
\hat{p} \pm z^* \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}
\]

Statistic: \(\hat{p}=x/n\)

SE: \(\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}\)

Conditions: SRS/random sample, independent outcomes, and large counts (\(n\hat{p} \ge 10\), \(n(1-\hat{p}) \ge 10\))




\[

\]

title: Quiz 9
text:
These practice problems cover Lectures 22–24 and Lab 9 (tidyverse):

Lecture 22: z-scores, standard normal probabilities, standardized comparisons

Lecture 23: standardized test statistic for a proportion, normal approximation, prop.test(), organizing analyses by variable type and purpose; numerical vs categorical variables

Lecture 24: mean and median

Lab 9: dplyr verbs and piping (filter, select, mutate, summarise/summarize, group_by, count, …)






Work without notes first, then check your work.
Try giving these problems to your favorate AI and ask it to teach you how to do the problems and then quiz you on them. You can upload a pdf using the “+” button in Chatgpt or try Google’s Notebook LM.







Suppose women’s heights in a certain population are approximately normal with mean \(\mu = 64\) inches and standard deviation \(\sigma = 2.5\) inches. Annarose is 67 inches tall. Compute her z-score relative to this population.




Newton is 72 inches tall. Men’s heights in his reference population are approximately normal with mean \(\mu = 70\) inches and \(\sigma = 3\) inches. Compute Newton’s z-score. Who is more unusually tall relative to their own group, Annarose (from A1) or Newton? (Hint: be sure to compare z-scores, not raw heights.)




Let \(Z\) be standard normal. Using the Standard Normal Table table, find \(P(Z \le 1.24)\). State what this probability represents (e.g. the proportion of values below \(z = 1.2\) under the standard normal curve). Also find \(P(Z > 1.24)\).







A coin is fair if the probability of heads is \(0.5\). You observe \(x = 16\) heads in \(n = 20\) flips. State \(H_0\) and \(H_a\) for a two-sided test of whether the coin is fair. Identify \(p\) (population proportion of heads) in words.




In Lecture 23, the standardized statistic for testing a population proportion has the form
\[
z = \frac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}}.
\]
What role does \(p_0\) play? (Choose one.)
a) It is the observed value of \(\hat{p}\) from the data.
b) It is the value of the population proportion or probability of a success in a single trial assumed under \(H_0\).
c) It is the sample size.
d) It is the standard deviation of the population.




In R, prop.test(x = 16, n = 20, p = 0.5) tests a proportion. If you are conducting a test of a null and alternative hypothesis, what value should you look for in the output to decide whether to reject the null hypothesis in favor of the alternative? How will you decide based on the size of this value?




Suppose you have one binary variable (yes/no) from a sample. Give one descriptive summary you might report and one inferential question you might use a confidence interval to answer. Give an example of an inferential question you could answer with a p-value from a **hypothesis** test.




For each variable below, state whether it is numerical or categorical. If it is categorical, also say whether it is binary (exactly two categories) or not binary (more than two categories). One line each.

Stem diameter (cm) measured for each tree in a sample.
Soil type recorded as sand, loam, or clay for each plot.
Parasite present recorded as yes or no for each captured animal.
Egg count (0, 1, 2, …) in each nest in a breeding survey.








Data: \(3,\ 5,\ 5,\ 7,\ 9\). Compute the mean and the median.







In R, x %>% f() means “take x and pass it as the first input to f().” What is the main purpose of the pipe in a tidyverse workflow? (Note: x |> f() has the same result)
a) To load the tidyverse package.
b) To chain steps: send the result of one step into the next step in a natural-feeling order.
c) To delete missing values from a data frame.
d) To convert a vector to a factor.




Which function keeps only the rows of a dataset that satisfy a logical condition such as hwy > 30?
a) select()
b) filter()
c) mutate()
d) summarise() (aka summarize())




Which function is used to choose a subset of columns (variables) from a dataset by name?
a) filter()
b) select()
c) pull()
d) arrange()




Which function is used to compute summary statistics (such as one mean or one median) from a dataset, often producing fewer rows than the original dataset?
a) mutate()
b) filter()
c) summarise() (aka summarize())
d) group_by()




Which function adds a new column to a dataset in a way that the new column contains new values but the same number of rows as the original dataset?
a) summarise()
b) count()
c) mutate()
d) summarise() with n()




Which function gives a count of how many rows fall into each category of a categorical variable?
a) count()
b) mutate()
c) group_by() alone
d) summarise() without group_by()




What is the role of group_by() when placed before summarise()?
a) It sorts the rows alphabetically.
b) It defines groups so that summarise() computes one summary number per group.
c) It drops all columns not listed.
d) It sorts the rows by the first column.




Inside summarise(), what does n() return?
a) The number of columns in the data frame.
b) The number of rows in the current group (or in the whole table if ungrouped).
c) The maximum value of a variable.
d) The name of the first column.

title: Course Syllabus
text: Welcome to STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics!

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 1

Monday
Holiday - No class


Wednesday
Lecture 1
Introduction — instructor, classmates, course, and what statistics is

Meet the teacher and your classmates.
Describe what STAT 109 covers and how lecture, lab, quizzes, and projects fit together.
Explain informally what statistics is and give examples of questions it can help answer.
Describe five main steps in the statistical process



Friday
Lecture 2
Probability as data vs theory

Compute counts, proportions, and percentages for an event across repeated trials.
Use symbols \(x\), \(n\), \(\hat{p}\), and \(P(\text{event})\) for data vs theoretical probability.
Distinguish observed outcomes from theoretical probability in a random process.
State three rules that probabilities must satisfy.
Define probability and connect it to long-run relative frequency.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 2

Monday
Lecture 3
Probability calculations the long way — sample spaces and events

Define and identify sample space, outcome, and event for a trial.
Use notation \(P(A)\) for the probability of an event.
State and use the formula for \(P(A)\) when outcomes in the sample space are equally likely.



Wednesday
Lecture 4
Random babies — probability by careful counting

List the sample space for randomly returning four babies to four mothers.
Count outcomes for events such as “all correct” or “exactly \(k\) correct.”
Compute probabilities when each pairing in the sample space is equally likely.
Compare exact probabilities to a simulation (e.g. Random Babies applet).



Friday
Lecture 5
Addition rule for probability

State the addition rule for any two events \(A\) and \(B\).
Use the addition rule to compute \(P(A \text{ or } B)\), including overlapping events.
Apply special cases: complement rule and non-overlapping events.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 3

Monday
Lecture 6
Conditional probability and multiplication rule

State the definition of conditional probability and compute \(P(A \mid B)\).
Use the multiplication rule to compute \(P(A \text{ and } B)\).
Decide whether two events are independent using equivalent criteria.



Wednesday
Lecture 7
Law of total probability and Bayes’ rule

State the law of total probability for a partition of the sample space.
State Bayes’ rule and relate the pieces to conditional and marginal probabilities.
Use tree diagrams to organize two-stage problems and find \(P(B)\) and \(P(A \mid B)\).



Friday
Lecture 8
Practice — Lectures 6–7 (diagnostic testing and Bayes)

Compute and interpret conditional probabilities from counts (e.g. sensitivity, false positive rate).
Assess independence in context and use the law of total probability for \(P(B)\).
Build tree diagrams and apply Bayes’ rule to find inverse probabilities such as \(P(C \mid T^+)\).

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 4

Monday
Lecture 9
Permutations and combinations

Distinguish settings where order matters (permutations) from where it does not (combinations).
Use \(_nP_r\) and \(_nC_r\) (or \(\binom{n}{r}\)) to solve counting problems.
Relate factorials to the permutation and combination formulas.



Wednesday
Lecture 10
From coin tosses to the binomial random process

Define a discrete random variable and its probability distribution (pdf).
Define a binomial random variable and a binomial random process.
Match notation (\(n\), \(x\), \(p\)) and the four conditions for a binomial process to a coin-toss setting.
Express \(P(X = x)\) using the binomial pdf.



Friday
Lecture 11
Practice — binomial random process

Decide whether a scenario satisfies the four binomial conditions and justify your answer.
Identify \(n\), \(p\), and the random variable \(X\) for a binomial count.
Write and evaluate probabilities using the binomial pdf; use mean \(\mu = np\) and SD \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\) as needed.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 5

Monday
Lecture 12
Introduction to the binomial test (ESP example)

Translate a research question about a proportion into \(H_0\) and \(H_a\).
Check the four BRP conditions for data from a fixed number of binary trials.
Connect the ESP activity to the structure of Project 1.



Wednesday
Lecture 13
ESP worksheet — binomial test in practice

State hypotheses and verify the BRP conditions for your own ESP test data.
Identify \(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\) and record your observed count \(x\).
Obtain a p-value from the sample R notebook and share results as directed.



Friday
Lecture 14
Binomial test — steps and worked examples

Carry out the full workflow: question, parameter, hypotheses, \(n\) and \(p\) under \(H_0\), BRP check, null distribution plot, observed \(x\), shading by \(H_a\), p-value, and conclusion in context.
Handle one-sided and two-sided alternatives correctly when shading and interpreting the p-value.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 6

Monday
Lecture 15
Mean and standard deviation of a binomial random variable

Use \(\mu = np\) and \(\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}\) for \(X \sim \text{Binomial}(n,p)\).
Interpret the mean as the center and the SD as typical deviation of \(X\).
Identify \(n\) and \(p\) in applied settings and compute \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\).



Wednesday
Lecture 16
Normal approximation to the binomial; one-proportion test by hand

Use the CLT to approximate a binomial distribution by a normal curve with the same \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\).
Check conditions such as \(np \geq 10\) and \(n(1-p) \geq 10\) when appropriate.
State the Empirical Rule for normal distributions.
Sketch a rejection region under a normal curve for a one-proportion test and relate the shaded area to the p-value (approximate).



Friday
Lecture 17
Type I and Type II errors; power

Define Type I and Type II errors and describe each in context.
Define power as \(1 - P(\text{Type II error})\) and relate it to detecting a false \(H_0\).
Explain how significance level \(\alpha\) and sample size help balance error rates and power.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 7

Monday
Lecture 18
Sampling distributions and estimation

Define the sampling distribution of a statistic.
Describe confidence level, parameter, and the endpoints of a confidence interval in context.
Compute and interpret a confidence interval for a population proportion and for a population mean (methods from lecture).



Wednesday
Review
Review before Midterm 1

Consolidate probability rules, counting, binomial models, and binomial tests.
Bring questions about practice problems and course structure for the exam.



Friday
Midterm 1
Midterm 1 (in lecture)

Demonstrate understanding of material covered through the review session (see course schedule and practice materials).

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 8

Monday
Lecture 19
Confidence intervals and the importance of sampling

Discuss bias and its impact given how a sample was collected.
Identify ways to reduce bias and increase precision.
Identify a sampling frame and carry out simple random sampling.
Classify methods as convenience, SRS, cluster, systematic, stratified, or multi-stage.



Wednesday
Lecture 20
Sampling methods in practice

Identify a sampling frame for a stated population.
Carry out simple random sampling using a numbered list and random draws.
Classify sampling designs and discuss bias, generalizability, and how to improve the design.



Friday
Lecture 21
Target & Rubberband Analogy

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 9

Monday
Lecture 22
Normal distribution and standardization

Compute a z-score from an observation and the mean and standard deviation of a reference distribution.
Interpret a z-score in context and use z-scores for standardized comparisons between groups or to a reference.
Find probabilities (areas) for the standard normal distribution and describe them as proportions of values below (or above) a cutoff.



Wednesday
Lecture 23
Organizing the statistics toolbox — z-test for a proportion, variable types, CI vs. testing

Compute a standardized test statistic using the normal approximation to the binomial and connect it to a one-proportion test (often called a z-test); in R, use prop.test() and read off a confidence interval as well.
Organize analyses by type of variable(s) and by purpose (e.g. description vs. inference).
Relate descriptive tools to inferential choices for one categorical variable (e.g. proportion, confidence interval vs. hypothesis test).



Friday
Lecture 24
One numerical variable — center, graphs, and the one-sample t-test

Compute mean and median by hand and with the tidyverse (pipe to summarize()).
Graph one numerical variable with ggplot2 using a histogram and a dot plot.
State the one-sample t-statistic and use t.test() for a one-sample t-test and confidence interval for the population mean.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 10

Monday
Lecture 25
One numerical outcome across two groups — spread, boxplots, and two-sample t

Compute range, quartiles, IQR, and standard deviation by hand and compare two small datasets.
Build and interpret boxplots as a picture of the five-number summary.
Describe the Welch two-sample t-test (independent groups, unequal variances), its t-statistic, degrees of freedom, hypotheses, and conditions; run t.test(y ~ group) in R.
Use group_by() with summarize() for descriptives and ggplot2 (fill or color) to compare groups with histograms, dot plots, or boxplots.



Wednesday
Lecture 26
Two numerical variables — scatterplots and describing relationships

Make a scatterplot in ggplot2 for two numerical variables (aes(x, y) and geom_point()).
Describe an association using form (e.g. linear vs curved), direction (positive, negative, or none), and strength (strong, moderate, weak, or none).



Friday
Lecture 27
Two categorical variables — contingency tables, row/column/total proportions, stacked bar charts

Build a two-way table of counts and compute joint, row, and column proportions (know which denominator you use).
Draw stacked bar charts for counts and 100% stacked bars (position = "fill") to show proportions within bars.
Implement tables and plots with count(), group_by(), mutate(), and pivot_wider().

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 11

Monday
Lecture 28
Inference for two-group comparisons — causal questions and study design

Compare randomized controlled trials and observational designs when the goal is a causal claim about a treatment or exposure.
Explain how participants end up in comparison groups in an RCT versus an observational study.
State what a p-value does and does not address for a difference between two groups.



Wednesday
Lecture 29
Confounding

Define a confounder in words and recognize plausible confounders in a stated observational comparison.
Explain how a confounder can create or distort an association between an explanatory variable and a response.
Describe subclassification / stratification when a confounder is measured.



Friday
Lecture 30
Study design, p-values, and two-group inference (Welch two-sample t-test, proportions, RCT examples)

Use the Methods table (Lecture 30) to pick t.test() for two independent numerical groups or prop.test() / chisq.test() for binary comparisons, and name key conditions (e.g. independence, expected counts).
Interpret Welch two-sample t-test output (hypotheses, p-value, confidence interval) in context.
Connect random assignment in an RCT to reduced risk of confounding for treatment–outcome comparisons.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 12

Monday
Lecture 31
Matched pairs — differences, plots, paired t-test, and McNemar for paired binary data

Identify a matched pairs design and compute within-pair differences (or use a paired test) when the same units contribute two measurements.
Summarize paired numerical data with plots (e.g. boxplot of differences) and run a paired t-test (or one-sample test on differences).
For two binary outcomes on the same units, build the right table and run McNemar’s test when appropriate.



Wednesday
Lecture 32
One-way ANOVA — comparing means across more than two groups

Recognize when the outcome is numerical and the explanatory factor has more than two levels, so a one-way ANOVA is the natural extension of a two-sample t-test.
State the omnibus null hypothesis for equal population means across groups.



Friday
Lecture 33
Chi-square tests — independence and goodness of fit

Build contingency tables of counts for two categorical variables and use chisq.test() as appropriate.
Check expected counts (and know when simulate.p.value = TRUE is needed).
Relate chi-square tests to the “by chance” explanation for a table.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 13

Monday
Lecture 34
Work session — Project 4 (simulation, notebook, and writeup)

Apply study-design ideas (RCT vs observational, confounding, DAG) to your assigned two-group question.
Produce a reproducible simulated dataset (set.seed) and a notebook with descriptives, plots, and the correct test/CI from the Methods table.
Draft or refine the one-page summary for a classmate (plan + sample results + link to runnable code).



Wednesday
Review
Review before Midterm 2

Consolidate two-group inference, study design, confounding, and the Methods table.
Bring questions about practice problems, Project 4, and the exam format.



Friday
Midterm 2
Midterm 2 (in lecture)

Demonstrate understanding of material covered through the Week 13 review (see course schedule and practice materials).

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 14

Monday
Lecture 35
Correlation and simple linear regression

Interpret a correlation coefficient (strength and direction) for two numerical variables.
Fit and interpret a simple linear regression line as a model for how the mean of \(Y\) changes with \(x\).
Use scatterplots and diagnostic thinking about linear vs curved form.



Wednesday
Lecture 36
Linear regression inference — confidence and prediction intervals

Interpret confidence intervals for the slope and for the mean response at a chosen value of \(x\).
Interpret prediction intervals for an individual future response and explain why they are wider than mean-response intervals.
Connect residual assumptions to validity of interval-based inference.



Friday
Lecture 37
Logistic regression — modeling a binary outcome

Recognize when the response is binary and a logistic (log-odds) model is appropriate instead of ordinary least squares regression.
Interpret fitted probabilities or odds ratios from simple logistic output in context (at the level taught in this course).
Connect logistic regression to earlier ideas about proportions and two-group comparisons.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Week 15

Monday
Lecture 38
Ethics and statistical reasoning review

Use a truth-first lens to connect design, assumptions, missingness, and communication.
Evaluate ethical pitfalls in generalization, causal language, reporting, and model validity.
Practice responsible statistical wording and evidence-based conclusions.



Wednesday
Review
Project 5 work day

Finalize team predictor choice, measurements, and data collection.
Begin/finish analysis workflow and draft the individual one-page summary.
Confirm team-member contribution statements.



Friday
Review
Project portfolio review (Projects 1-5)

Synthesize methods and conclusions across all five projects.
Identify strongest evidence statements and common interpretation pitfalls.
Prepare final revisions for Project 4 and Project 5 submissions.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
R Software Downloads
STAT 109 uses R for statistical computing. You can use R in several ways:

Base R
Download R: R Project Website
R is free and open-source statistical software. The base R installation includes the core R language and basic packages.

Windows: Download the installer from CRAN (Comprehensive R Archive Network)
Mac: Download the macOS installer from CRAN
Linux: Install via your distribution’s package manager or from CRAN



RStudio
Download RStudio: RStudio Desktop
RStudio is an integrated development environment (IDE) for R that provides a user-friendly interface, code editor, and helpful features for data analysis. RStudio Desktop is free and works with base R.
Note: You must install base R first before installing RStudio.


R in Google Colab
Access R in Google Colab: R in Colab
Google Colab provides free access to R in a cloud-based notebook environment. This is useful if you don’t want to install R on your computer or need to work from different devices.


R in Jupyter Notebook
Install R for Jupyter: IRkernel Documentation
Jupyter Notebook is another notebook environment that supports R. To use R in Jupyter:

Install base R first
Install Jupyter Notebook or JupyterLab
Install the IRkernel package in R
Register the kernel with Jupyter

Note: R is also installed on all campus computers, so you can use the computer labs if you prefer not to install software on your personal computer.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Course Description (Catalog Description)
Descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, discrete and continuous distributions, confidence intervals, contingency tests, regression and correlation, tests of hypothesis, analysis of variance. Emphasis: methods and applications used in the biological and natural resource sciences.
Division: Lower Division  Weekly: Lecture 3 hrs. Activity 2 hrs.  Instruction Mode: In-person
Prerequisite: MATH 101 or MATH 101I or MATH 102 or MATH 109B (may be taken concurrently with instructor approval) with a grade of C- or better or ALEKS PPL assessment score over 65 or appropriate high school coursework. See Math Placement and ALEKS PPL for details. MATH 102 may be taken concurrently with instructor approval.
Note: if you plan to use STAT 109 as a prerequisite for another MATH, DATA or STAT course, you must have a grade of C- or better in this course.

Course Learning Outcomes
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

Use numerical and graphical descriptive statistics to summarize and interpret data.
Explain the role of randomness, probability, and sampling in statistical inference.
Apply probability rules and work with common probability distributions.
Construct and interpret confidence intervals for means and proportions.
Conduct and interpret basic hypothesis tests.
Analyze relationships between pairs of variables using correlation and regression.
Use a statistical programming language to analyze data and communicate results.
Distinguish between correlation and causation and recognize limitations of statistical conclusions.



General Education & Institutional Learning Outcomes
This course fulfills Lower Division General Education Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning) and supports Cal Poly Humboldt’s Institutional Learning Outcomes related to quantitative reasoning, critical thinking, and communication.

Access & Affordability
Students who do not have personal access to required technology may use campus computer labs and library resources. If you want to get a free used computer, please check to see if our campus’s Reuseable Office Supply Exchange (ROSE) has one available.

title: Course Syllabus
text:
Late or Missed Work Policies
The policies for late or missed work are as follows:

Daily attendance in lecture is required, but I know that sometimes you will need to miss class due to illness or other emergencies. Each student may miss up to 10 days of lecture without penalty and without contacting me. It’s up to you to reserve these days for emergencies only. In my experience, it is rare for a student to succeed if they regularly miss lecture or are absent for more than two weeks so if you find yourself in this situation, please communicate with me and your advisor so we can help you find a solution to getting back on track.
Weekly quizzes during Thursday lab sessions are required, but I know that sometimes you will need to miss lab due to illness or other emergencies. If this happens, please contact me to make an arrangement to take the quiz at another time.
Weekly programming labs are due Tuesdays in Canvas. After the due date, a solution will be posted, so late submissions are generally not accepted except in documented emergencies. These labs will be graded on completion and your reflection.
Projects are due on the specified date in Canvas. You may submit a late project up to a week late with no penalty. After a week, a project can be turn in for 70% of the grade.
Midterms are required. If you need to miss a midterm due to illness or other emergencies, please communicate with me as soon as possible to reschedule it. Alternatively, you may take the optional final exam (Wednesday, May 13, 2026, 10:20–12:10 PM, BSS 166) to replace a missed midterm grade.

Note: Technology issues (internet access, device failure, software problems) are not automatically considered valid excuses for late or missed work; students are expected to plan accordingly.


AI Policy
Much of your course grade will be based on materials that you produce in-class without having AI as a resource. But you may use AI, such as NotebookLM or ChatGPT, in this course to help you learn the materials or to perform data analyses or on the projects.
For work that you do at home that is turned in for a grade I have two requirements:

if you used AI, acknowledge this briefly in the document (you don’t need to state in detail how you used AI or give your prompts, just the name of the AI service you used).

Be responsible for the correctness and appropriateness of all (AI assisted or not) materials you turn in.

I may use AI to assist me in my delivery of the course material, such as asking AI to see if an assignment is appropriate for a college level course or if the wording of a quiz question could be improved. I will not use AI to grade your work or use an AI detection tool to check your work.

Lectures: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00-11:50 AM with warm-ups in the first 5 minutes of class
Labs: Get help on the code for your programming lab on Thursday (select one section: 9:00-10:50 AM, 11:00-12:50 PM, or 1:00-2:50 PM), then turn into Canvas by the following Tuesday. Elements of a lab may appear on the quiz, in addition to questions from that week’s practice problem set.

This class is accompanied by a 1-unit Supplemental Instruction course designed to assist students in better understanding course materials. There is room for 20 students within the SI course, and it can be accessed through the normal enrollment process.
